data.title: LVMH: Scarcity Sells In An Age Of Abundance
data.description:
Introduction

LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF) the world’s largest luxury-goods conglomerate, and it keeps proving its resilience in an unsettled global economy. The group’s portfolio of prestigious “maisons” (from fashion flagships Louis Vuitton and Dior to jeweler Tiffany & Co.) thrives by balancing supply with demand. LVMH practices controlled scarcity, limiting distribution, rolling out small, tightly edited capsule collections and using waitlists to heighten anticipation for must-have pieces. The result is a level of exclusivity that lets the company set prices on its terms across every division. In short, LVMH’s entire investment story hangs on that scarcity playbook: by making less, the group earns more, posting sturdy margins and steady expansion even when the broader economy stumbles.

Controlled Scarcity Safeguards Brand Equity

At Louis Vuitton and Christian Dior, LVMH’s flagship fashion maisons, products are intentionally kept scarce and distribution is tightly controlled. These brands never chase volume at the expense of exclusivity; they limit sales largely to their own boutiques, avoid broad discounting, and often release limited-edition capsule collections that sell out quickly. Louis Vuitton’s revival of its Takashi Murakami collaboration after twenty years shows the strategy in action: the bags were produced in small numbers and sold out on pent-up demand. Dior likewise runs waitlists for new handbags rather than flooding stores. Because demand outruns supply, both labels maintain the aura of rarity that supports premium pricing and healthy profit margins. Crucially, this approach eliminates the need for markdowns or outlet sales, preserving full-price integrity and reinforcing each brand’s luxury image.

Scarcity Proves Its Worth in Tough Times

Scarcity gives LVMH a cushion when the economy wobbles. Because products are hard to find, shoppers who can afford them usually keep buying at full price instead of waiting for discounts. When demand cools, the company doesn’t slash prices. It simply makes fewer items, keeping margins healthy and the brands’ image pristine. The first quarter of 2025 proved the value of that approach: organic sales slipped a modest three percent while many mass-market retailers fell much harder. Target’s comparable sales dropped 3.8%, Kohl’s net sales fell 4.1%, and fast-fashion giant H&M warned of a weak start to spring after only a 1% uptick in March sales. Investors prize that kind of consistency, rewarding LVMH with a valuation premium few rivals can match.

Engineered Scarcity Across Segments

Fashion & Leather Goods: This largest business group exemplifies LVMH’s volume-versus-value philosophy. In Q1 2025 Fashion & Leather Goods revenue was €10.1 billion, down 5 percent organically from the exceptional post-pandemic surge a year earlier, yet that dip still counted as “good resilience,” achieved with no broad promotions. Louis Vuitton and Dior kept demand strong by rolling out fresh collections. Dior’s newest handbags launched with solid sales even though production was intentionally limited.

Watches & Jewelry: Short runs and familiar flagship lines kept shoppers engaged, and revenue stayed roughly level year over year despite a tough 2024 comparison. Bulgari and Chaumet focused on tightly controlled batches of high-end pieces that carry healthy margins.

In timepieces, TAG Heuer and Hublot stirred collectors’ interest with special editions, while Tiffany & Co., the newest name in the portfolio, expanded its signature lines into more cities worldwide.

Selective Retailing: This segment (Sephora, DFS, department stores) held up well, with organic revenue off only 1 percent. Sephora grew on a high base by sticking to a premium assortment and standout in-store service. Le Bon Marché posted “a good start to the year,” helped by exclusive events and curated products.

Financials

Q1 2025 revenue landed at €20.3 billion, a modest 3 percent organic decline from Q1 2024’s exceptional showing. Wines & Spirits dropped the most at 9 percent organic, while Fashion & Leather Goods slid 5 percent. Perfumes & Cosmetics and Selective Retailing were roughly flat, and Watches & Jewelry was stable. This mix of soft spots and stable areas shows the business still holding its ground against last year’s high bar. LVMH did not report quarterly profit, but its margins have historically sat near record highs, and full-price selling likely kept them that way. Strong brands and tight cost control usually turn revenue into cash quickly because inventory moves at healthy margins. Management says it is “both vigilant and confident” for the rest of 2025, pointing to innovation, brand spending, and selective distribution as the main growth drivers. Management’s tone implies a high-single-digit operating-income increase for 2025, predicated on a second-half stabilization in China and a low-single-digit rebound in the United States.

Risk

Even a powerhouse like LVMH faces hazards. A sharp downturn in global luxury demand could hit spending on high-end goods more than pent-up demand can offset. Bain & Company estimates mainland China’s personal-luxury market contracted 18–20% in 2024 and may remain roughly flat in 2025. That slowdown already appears in LVMH’s figures: first-quarter revenue from Asia (excluding Japan) fell 11%, more than twice the group average. Bain expects mid-single-digit growth to resume in 2026 as Beijing’s stimulus and a stabilizing property sector rebuild confidence. Chinese consumers already represent about one-quarter of global luxury spending and could reach 35–40% by 2030, so an extended malaise would dent LVMH’s top line even if scarcity supports margins. The Wall Street Journal likewise warns that simultaneous softness in China and the U.S., together about half of luxury demand, could drag sector revenue down 2% this year.

Reliance on Chinese shoppers is another swing factor: any economic slowdown or travel curbs in China would pose a headwind. Results are also concentrated in two maisons, Louis Vuitton and Dior, which generate a disproportionate share of profit; a misstep at either label would weigh on group numbers. Finally, the stock’s premium valuation could magnify share-price volatility if growth disappoints. Investors should be ready for occasional bumps on an otherwise smooth road.

Conclusion

LVMH’s first quarter of 2025 showed that its scarcity-first strategy keeps the group strong even in a tricky climate. The deliberate supply limits at Louis Vuitton, Dior, and other maisons are more than marketing tricks; they form the backbone of brand equity and margins. This approach has cultivated deep customer loyalty and firm pricing power. This gives LVMH a resilience few companies can match when the economy softens. Sales stayed strong after last year’s surge as a result of fresh product launches, selective distribution, and hands-on oversight at each maison. For investors, the company provides steady cash flow and dependable returns while continually refreshing its brands. Its collection of iconic labels, built up over decades, forms a moat that rivals struggle to cross.

Real luxury feeds on scarcity, and LVMH knows this better than anyone. With that edge intact, the company appears ready to stay ahead of the field for years to come. Assuming management delivers the high-single-digit operating-income growth it has implicitly signaled, I see organic sales dipping a further 2–3% in 2025 and rebounding to roughly +5 % in 2026 as Chinese demand stabilizes and U.S. spending firms. This would pull the forward P/E below 18 and could restore double-digit annual returns. At a market value of about $277 billion, the shares still look reasonably priced.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: If Tariffs Don't Stick, Try Taxes
data.description:
Wall Street limped into the weekend like a diplomat on day five of a failed summit. Sure, inflation is inching toward 2% like the Holy Grail wrapped in red tape, but there’s a strong sense that someone, somewhere, will soon impose a tax.

Level Change 5/30/25 (%)

+0.1 Dow

-0.3 Nasdaq

-0.1 Nasdaq 100

-0.0 S&P 500

-0.4 S&P 400

-0.4 S&P 600

Despite Friday’s fade, May ended on a high note. The S&P and Nasdaq logged their best month since November 2023 - up 6.2% and 9.6%, respectively - fueled by AI exuberance, a few economic reprieves, and a US tariff policy that turned out less fearsome than feared. It helps that “Trump always chickens out” - the TACO trade.

That could change.

Trade once again hogged the spotlight, with President Trump jumping on Truth Social to accuse China of dragging its feet on tariff rollbacks. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent echoed the frustration, calling talks “a bit stalled,” though he dangled the prospect of a Trump-Xi call. China, for its part, declined to confirm or deny the existence of Donald Trump.

USTR Jamieson Greer said Beijing removed tariffs as promised but is dragging its feet on non-tariff countermeasures, like rare earth restrictions and blacklisting US firms. That’s a problem when your green economy needs Chinese magnets to stay, well, magnetic. If only someone had thought this through in advance. Maybe, I don’t know... planned?

And just when you thought this trade war couldn’t get more inventive, along comes Section 899, the latest weapon from our Beltway Bureau of Unintended Consequences. The so-called “capital war clause” in the “One Big Beautiful Bill” would hike US taxes on investors from “discriminatory” countries. Think France, with its 3% digital services tax aimed squarely at US megatech, or Germany, reportedly weighing a 10% version of its own.

That could weaken foreign demand for Treasuries, just when the US needs every bond buyer it can get. As Deutsche Bank warned, “weaponization of US capital markets into law” may sound patriotic, but does risk spooking the very investors we count on to fund the deficit.

Well, at least the Fed got a rare win.

April’s core PCE rose just 0.1% month-over-month, pushing the year-over-year rate to a post-2021 low of 2.5%. It was the second straight cooler print and lent some credence to the idea that disinflation isn’t a myth after all, even if tariffs haven’t shown up in the numbers yet. No, it’s not enough to pry the Fed from its wait-and-see wall stare, but we can leave the Eccles smelling salts in the drawer.

Costco (COST +3.1%) rode consumer anxiety to a gain, touting tariff workarounds and the timeless appeal of bulk peanut butter. Dell (DELL -2.1%) missed on EPS but dazzled with $7B in AI server shipments and an order book stuffed to the gills, proving that the next best thing to printing money is being Nvidia (NVDA)-adjacent.

And with that, May closed on a cautious high: tariffs unresolved, tax threats rising, inflation cooling, and AI still gunning for humanity’s user manual.

June arrives Monday. Brace for another chapter in the saga titled Economic Policy as Performance Art.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Where To Look For Opportunities As America Remakes Its Economy
data.description:
Concerns about on again, off again tariff talk, as well as large transformative tax reforms under consideration in the U.S. Congress, are helping to fuel market uncertainty. Michael Craig, Managing Director and Head of Asset Allocation and Derivatives at TD Asset Management, discusses the risks and potential opportunities as investors navigate this highly complex landscape.

Transcript

Kim Parlee - From on-again, off-again tariff talks to bond market concerns about the Big, Beautiful Bill making its way through the US Congress, there are certainly lots of factors weighing on the markets these days. So how should investors navigate these highly complex landscapes? Michael Craig is Managing Director and Head of Asset Allocation and Derivatives at TD Asset Management, and he's here to walk us through the risks and opportunities and how he thinks. Hello.

Michael Craig - Hi, Kim. How are you doing?

Kim Parlee - I'm doing well. I think I just almost want to start there and just say, the markets have roared back. Equity markets have roared right back up. And often, in let's say even the semis and the tech and we've seen lots of things move back up. Bond markets, I think, are a little less sure perhaps of where we're going. How do you think about where we are right now in the equity and bond markets?

Michael Craig - So we still have a modest overweight in equities. We've started to cover a little bit of our fixed income underweight, as the yields have risen quite high. There was news overnight in Japan about them withdrawing some issuance because the Japanese market has actually been ground -- the bond market's actually been ground zero for the move in yields.

And so bonds feel like this move higher is probably near the end. If not, we'll have a little bit of a rally. But we're still going back and forth between kind of is Trump in a good mood today or is he in a bad mood today? And this constant debate or negotiation, in terms of what trade looks like in the years to come.

I think with the tariff side, we're probably getting to the end of the tariff story. But make no mistake, this isn't the end of what they're trying to do and accomplish and to remake what America looks like. And what I find -- what's really interesting is the reaction amongst other foreign capitals in terms of what they're going to do. I find that quite fascinating. And for us, it is starting to open up opportunities to look for investments in other jurisdictions outside of the US.

Kim Parlee - Can you tell me a bit more about that, what's fascinating?

Michael Craig - Well, we've kind of looked -- the world right now is selling their wares to the US consumer and taking those dollars and reinvesting them into the US financial system, whether it's in the Treasury market, equity market, private markets. I think this is about to change. I think that foreign capitals - Canada, Europe, Asia, et cetera - are going to look for ways to stimulate growth that isn't as reliant on the US.

They're going to be looking to become more self-sufficient. You heard it in the throne speech today. Many things that Carney or King Charles talked about was leading to more growth in Canada.

Kim Parlee - Production, independence, right?

Michael Craig - Production, independence, what have you. And so this is very different than what we've been used to, and is going to lead to, I think, some indigestion in markets and other opportunities. And so we've moved more capital outside of North America. We've moved more capital to Canada, trying to really diversify our equity mix. There's a big valuation gap still persistent. And I think this is going to lead to better growth prospects in the world, ex-US, when we look back in a couple of years.

Kim Parlee - Is this stagflationary? I think that's what people worry about.

Michael Craig - It's not. I think that's -- near term might be the case. But the stagflation is weak growth, high inflation. So weak growth we get from uncertainty, which I think on the trade side is going to abate. And you saw the US Conference Board show much better expectations of the current situation today in today's data.

And on the inflation side, whatever tariff rate comes through, it's a one-time hit. It's like a tax. It's not something that's going to lead to more demand over supply continuously. So I think there's a short-term stagflation hit. I don't think stagflation is going to be in the lexicon of markets in six months from now.

Kim Parlee - Can you talk to us a bit about just yields in terms of what we've seen with the 30-year and the 10-year? And people make much ado about it crossing certain thresholds like 5% and what that means. Does it mean what we think it means? Or does it mean something different?

Michael Craig - Well, it's sometimes when you see higher yields, it's a reflection of better growth.

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Michael Craig - That would be a mistake. In the US, there has been some issue with deficits. The US is having to raise an extra $2 trillion a year. And if you take what I just recently said and take it to the next level, if countries now -- if there's more of a global demand for capital, if Canada wants to do infrastructure and Germany wants to do defense, et cetera, then the cost of capital has got to go higher. And that's going to be reflected in the bond market.

So I think the press is really hyping up the US deficit story. And it is a problem, no question. But I think what's much more interesting is that countries are now starting to look at that capital, that excess savings in their own country that's being exported and saying, we'd like some of that. Because we'd like to have our own. We have our own objectives.

And so I don't see a precipitous increase in yields. But I also think that Canadian 10-year rates shouldn't be at 3%. They should probably be more like 3.5% if we were able to raise our growth profile like the Liberals want to do. So I -- with fixed income right now, I mean, yields are actually pretty attractive.

The movement in yields is uncomfortable. But the all-in yield you're earning from coupon is not bad. But we're going to a level where investors are demanding higher and higher rates. And I don't think that we're going anywhere near where we were in the previous decade, back to 2%, 1% yields. I think those days are over. So if you're hoping for this big drop in yields, if you're looking to refinance, I would caution against that.

Kim Parlee - What does it mean for US dollar as reserve currency? This comes up every five seconds.

Michael Craig - There's a tactical answer, the strategic answer to that. Tactically, let's just say the next six weeks.

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Michael Craig - The market is very bearish, the dollar. And so today it had a bit of a move. I wouldn't expect to see a bit of a lift in the dollar. But once we get through that strategically, I think all roads lead to a weaker dollar. I just look at this set-up and everyone's talking about it. I don't think anyone's positioned for it.

And some of the things -- as we move away from trade, there's no chatter within the US administration about taxing assets in the US. There's a lot of tax treaties that we don't pay tax on private or public investments in the US. They're now looking at that. And that is the other side of trade.

I mean, there might be a trade imbalance, but it's also a capital imbalance. And if that's the direction they go, dollar is going a lot lower. And I would caution investors, we've had a bit of a free lunch last many years. Last 10 years, about 4% per year of US returns has come from Canadian dollar depreciation.

Kim Parlee - Yes.

Michael Craig - That's a lot. I don't think that tailwind exists anymore.

Kim Parlee - It's a headwind now.

Michael Craig - It's a headwind. And I think with investor portfolios, I wouldn't say, we have lots of US assets, but we're looking to rebalance that away. Because I'm mindful that I think that dollar is going to be a real headwind over the next handful of years.

Kim Parlee - I've only got 30 seconds for this part, but I want to ask about President Trump. But the last time we spoke, you mentioned that you thought he was expending a lot of political capital on the tariffs with the ordinary American. Do you have any thoughts? How do you think about his presidency from an investment standpoint?

Michael Craig - They got to get off the tariff issue quick or they're going to get -- or the midterms is going to be really messy for them. So that's why even -- Europe's the last bastion where he was puffing his chest and saying, we're doing 50. And then they walked that back, and they pushed that deadline forward.

Like I said, I don't think he's got the time to keep this issue going. When you look at voter preference in the US, it's right at the bottom of what the general American voter cares about. And a lot of his base, particularly those who are really sensitive to increasing costs, are going to be hurt by higher costs. So I think it's -- like I said, I think by the end of the summer, they've moved on to something else.

That's why I'm worried that they're going to look at foreign assets because they're not voters. And if you're -- someone's going to pay for this imbalance. And that's where he might turn to.

Kim Parlee - You talked a little bit about being modest overweight on equities. And so I wouldn't mind just talking about each asset and tell us where you think. And then finish up with the Canadian dollar. Tell me a bit more about within equities, what's you're -- where you're at?

Michael Craig - So we still like -- we like financials, particularly on the deregulation. And I think I mentioned earlier, I think the tariff story is later innings. And the deregulation story hasn't even started yet. The US financial system has arguably overregulated itself as a response of '08. And there's a lot of regulation that will likely lead to release of bank capital.

So we like the financials. We like industrials in terms of the focus on build in America. We've got positions in Europe as well as in adding to Canada. Again, valuations are more attractive. You get lower growth. But my argument earlier about these countries looking for investments actually should lead to higher growth profiles.

So still like equities, but are a bit more mindful and probably more of a trading mode than a strategically long and just let it ride mode, if you will. On bonds, we've had a material underweight, probably one of the largest we've had in a long time. We started to take a bit of that back. It has been the right call. Bond yields have moved higher.

But at these levels now, we're starting to add a bit to fixed income from cash. And then continue to build out in the private side. Infrastructure and commercial mortgages are a particular areas of interest. In terms of adding resilience to our portfolios, we absolutely don't believe that we're out of the woods in terms of the high levels of volatility, although April was probably the peak knock on wood volatility.

But I think there's still 3.5 years left in this presidency. So there's still likely periods of market turmoil. And my point, if they do look at putting forward taxes on foreign ownership, that could be quite negative. So not getting too carried away, but trying to really maximize the diversification of our portfolios right now.

Kim Parlee - I'm just kind of mentally trying to balance, net it out with, you say, foreign ownership. But alt assets, private infrastructure. I think all the buildout happening is quite positive. But is there something you have to be careful of with what you just said?

Michael Craig - Yeah, sure. I mean, if you've got US infrastructure, just like anything, there is a risk that you could see taxes. Now, again, the argument is that they're going to target countries where they feel they've got unfair tax policy that's hurting the US. In Canada, our digital services tax has been a thorny issue.

Kim Parlee - Which we saw in this Big, Beautiful Bill.

Michael Craig - Yeah. And so this is where, again, Trump starts with a big position then tries to negotiate from there. So I have no way to think that this is going to become law. But it's a risk on the horizon. It's in his mind. And so it's something to be mindful of, in terms of how it would hit us.

Kim Parlee - You mentioned that you're underweight -- modestly underweight the US dollar. What does that mean for the Canadian dollar? Does that mean that you see it going up?

Michael Craig - So I was on a panel last month and this came up. And the question was, we're looking at it inverse, so is the Canadian dollar going to sell off to 145 or rally to 130? My view is that, forget about that. Is it going back to 120 or 110? 120 would be $0.80 and 110 would be kind of low $0.90s over time.

Afterwards, I spoke with one of the economists from another bank. And he said the other day, I got to where you're thinking. So I'm not alone in this view. I think that as the flow of capital starts to slow into the US market, and if we are able to generate better growth here-- and interprovincial trade is like a real easy one.

Kim Parlee - It's real.

Michael Craig - It's real and it's right away positive to growth. It has actually major impacts to our growth. I mean, IMF has calculated for every 10% drop in inter-provincial trade barriers, it would add about 20 basis points to GDP.

So this could be a huge positive. I put those two things together. And I don't see any reason why the Canadian dollar should be this weak. So I think the CAD is going to be stronger. I think 120 would be my target over the next few years, if we -- execution is everything. So there's always that risk. But if we do execute on it, then that's where I think it's going.

Kim Parlee - I'm thinking about Canadian business people. I mean stronger loonie, better for buying things. But then again, tariff and all that good stuff. And everything kind of balances out.

Michael Craig - Yeah, but I mean, we've gotten really -- as a country, we've gotten pretty sloppy about our trade. We've way over-relied on the US market. 73% of our exports go there. We can only send oil one way. I mean, there's a lot of things where there's a low-hanging fruit for us to better balance our trade portfolio in terms of who we trade with.

This is well-recognized. And as you saw today, it is the focus of the government. I also think it's interesting that Carney has sent a single letter to all his ministers, the same letter of the objectives of the government. They are laser focused on growth and improving productivity in this country. So if anything else, I think they're going to be successful there.

Original Post

Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. NAV return data includes investment management fees, custodial charges and administrative fees (such as Director and legal fees) and assumes the reinvestment of all distributions. abrdn Inc. (the “Adviser”) became the Fund’s adviser on October 27, 2023.

For periods prior to that date, the returns reflect performance information from a prior, unaffiliated adviser. The Fund is subject to investment risk, including the possible loss of principal. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized.

The top three detractors were Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT), Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) and Vaxcyte (PCVX). Sarepta underperformed after reporting a patient death from acute liver failure linked to Elevidys, its gene therapy for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy — a development that raised significant investor concern. Regeneron declined amid continued pressure from the anticipated launch of generic competitors to Eyelea, its leading ophthalmology therapy. Vaxcyte also weakened after the release of mixed clinical data on its pneumococcal vaccine for children, compounded by broader uncertainty around regulatory and government vaccination policies.

The top three contributors were Amgen (AMGN), Gilead Sciences (GILD) and Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX). Amgen rebounded strongly despite a weak end to 2024, with its shares rising even amid mixed sentiment around its GLP-1 antibody, maritide — an impressive move given broader equity market softness. Gilead gained on growing optimism for its twice-yearly HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis drug, which has demonstrated near-complete efficacy and strengthened investor confidence. Vertex advanced significantly, led by the Food and Drug Administration’s approval of Journayvx — the first non-opioid acute pain treatment approved in over two decades — alongside continued robust demand for its cystic fibrosis therapies.

Market review

US equities ended the quarter notably down. Against a backdrop of lingering inflationary pressures and a relatively buoyant labour market, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25–4.50% during the quarter. The central bank’s chair, Jerome Powell, reiterated the Fed’s commitment to sustainably lowering annual inflation – as measured by its preferred measure, the core Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index – below the 2% target. However, the Fed aims to proceed cautiously, taking a data-dependent approach as it seeks greater clarity on underlying economic trends given persistently high inflation data and the potential inflationary impact of President Trump’s policies, particularly his tariff announcements. Despite this, following some softer US economic data, investors are now factoring in the possibility that rates could be cut at a slightly faster pace than previously expected.

The performance of the US healthcare sector notably outpaced that of the S&P 500 Index over the quarter. This was due to the relatively defensive nature of healthcare stocks, along with other sectors such as utilities and real estate that also outperformed. Investor risk aversion increased amid concerns that President Trump’s recent tariff announcements could prove inflationary and weigh on US economic growth. There were also heightened geopolitical tensions due to the ongoing wars in Ukraine and the Middle East. The energy sector outperformed too, supported by its perceived role as an inflation hedge, the Trump administration’s ‘energy dominance’ policy, and heightened geopolitical tensions. Materials also fared well, as fears of a potential trade war and geopolitical uncertainty supported gold prices. Elsewhere, the consumer discretionary sector was the main laggard, reflecting mounting concerns about the health of the US consumer. Information technology also notably underperformed, as the prospect of interest rates staying higher for longer weighed on the sector, given its large weighting to growth companies whose future discounted earnings would be negatively affected.

Activity

During the first quarter of 2025, we made several changes to the portfolio. Generally speaking, the portfolio management team identified relative value differentials favoring smaller-cap equities over large-cap pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies. Additionally, concerns over pending tariffs led us to adopt a more cautious stance toward the large-cap pharmaceutical and biotechnology sector. Consequently, we initiated several positions in predominantly small- and mid-cap pre-commercial or early product launch companies. Examples of new initiations include Incyte (INCY), Arbutus Biopharma (ABUS), Immunic (IMUX), Cidara Therapeutics (CDTX), ARS Pharmaceuticals (SPRY), Centessa Pharmaceuticals (CNTA), Mereo BioPharma (MREO), NewAmsterdam Pharma (NAMS), Tempus AI (TEM), Korro Bio (KRRO), and Sionna Therapeutics (SION). We also made a new investment in a pre-public, venture-backed company, Abcuro.

We sold several positions for various reasons. Some sales were motivated by a lack of confidence in the companies’ forward growth prospects, while others resulted from failed clinical trials. Over the past several quarters, we have been increasing the Fund’s allocation to smaller-cap equities and decreasing its allocation to large-cap equities; as a result, we sold some positions fitting these criteria. Examples of sold positions include Biogen (BIIB), Eli Lilly (LLY), Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), Intra-Cellular Therapies (ITCI), Natera (NTRA), BeiGene, Avantor (AVTR), 89bio (ETNB), Moderna (MRNA), AstraZeneca (AZN), and Marinus Pharmaceuticals.

Outlook & strategy

With Donald Trump securing his second term, uncertainty around election results has been replaced with policy ambiguity. Tax cuts and deregulation are supportive of GDP growth, but incremental tariffs and immigration restrictions could mute these gains. This combination of looser fiscal policy coupled with potential supply-side shocks raises inflation expectations. As a result, we expect Fed rate cuts to slow in 2025 while we await increased visibility over the policy agenda and its resulting impact on inflation.

In general, the near- and long-term outlooks for healthcare companies remain favourable. Long-term demographic trends of an aging population should continue to support the growing demand for new healthcare products and therapies. Due to the healthcare sector’s generally defensive characteristics, its relative underperformance in the short term should position it favourably, especially if the US and global economies slow down.

Footnote

[1] The NASDAQ Biotechnology Index is a modified capitalization-weighted index that includes securities of NASDAQ-listed companies classified according to the Industry Classification Benchmark as biotechnology or pharmaceuticals.

Important Information

Closed-end funds are traded on the secondary market through one of the stock exchanges. The Fund’s investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares may be worth more or less than the original cost. Shares of closed-end funds may trade above (a premium) or below (a discount) the net asset value (NAV) of the Fund. data.title: Tesla: Despite 'Woke Mob,' Musk To Better U.S. Again
data.description:
We've all been the target of anger, but few as much as Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) CEO Elon Musk, after he purchased Twitter and joined forces with President Donald Trump. However, an examination of his body of work at Tesla reveals he brilliantly created the modern electric vehicle ("EV") industry in anticipation of renewable energy becoming a significantly larger portion of the U.S. electric grid (see chart below) thereby enabling EVs to ultimately be powered more by clean energy and less by the dirty fossil fuels that dominated the grid and powered EVs when that industry began. It was a brilliant innovation that improved quality of life in the U.S., and he seems, presciently, about to do it again.

This report is about the existential total addressable market ("TAM") opportunity Full Self-Driving ("FSD") technology creates for Tesla, including competitive advantages, valuation, risks and concluding with my strong opinion on investing.

The Importance of Innovation:

Before getting into the details, and to briefly provide some high-level perspective, Warren Buffett is rumored to have once said about investing:

“First come the innovators, who see opportunities that others don’t. Then come the imitators, who copy what the innovators have done. And then come the idiots, whose avarice undoes the very innovations they are trying to use to get rich.”

Musk innovated the modern EV industry, many other companies around the globe are now imitating it, and as the market gets saturated, a new batch of third "i's" will continue to try to compete. Meanwhile, Musk has set his eyes on a new innovation; another very large market opportunity where his business can scale wide and far, quickly.

Understanding Full Self-Driving

Autopilot and FSD are Tesla’s driver-assistance technologies. They currently operate in “supervised mode” (which requires driver oversight); however Tesla’s goal is “unsupervised mode” (i.e., where the vehicles drive themselves entirely). This technology and goal has the potential to unlock a massive robotaxi network opportunity (and potentially transform the entire transportation industry as we know it). And Tesla expects a small fleet (10 to 20) of its full self-driving vehicles to begin hitting the roads in Austin, Texas within the next few days.

Estimated Total Addressable Market for FSD

The TAM for FSD is massive, considering the global transportation market’s size (think taxis, busses, trains and more) and FSD’s potential to disrupt it. For example, the ride-hailing market alone (think Uber (UBER) and Lyft (LYFT)) is projected to reach nearly $250 billion by 2030, with autonomous vehicles potentially capturing a significant share (due to lower operating costs). And if Tesla deploys a robotaxi network, some estimates suggest a TAM of $1–2 trillion globally for autonomous ride-hailing by 2040 (due to reduced labor costs and increased vehicle utilization).

Tesla’s existing fleet of over 5 million vehicles (already equipped with FSD capacity via software update), combined with its ability to scale production (faster and more efficiently than competitors—more on this in a moment), positions Tesla to potentially dominate this massive market opportunity. Further, potential FSD subscriptions and licensing to other manufacturers could add billions in recurring revenue (at a $99–$199 monthly subscription rate).

Tesla’s Competitive Edge Over Uber

Tesla has a big advantage over Uber (the current dominant ride hailing service) in the increasingly imminent autonomous market. Specifically, Uber relies on human drivers (who account for ~70% of ride costs), while Tesla’s FSD may eliminate driver costs entirely.

Further, Tesla’s vertically integrated approach (Tesla controls hardware, software, and data collection) gives it a dramatic advantage. For example, its vehicles generate real-time driving data and thereby update and refine FSD’s training models faster than competitors. On the other hand, Uber’s autonomous efforts (such as its partnership with Waymo) depend on third-party technologies, thereby limiting control and scalability (whereas Tesla’s fleet is already FSD-capable via software updates). Moreover, Tesla’s brand loyalty (arguably) and EV expertise enhance its competitive advantage.

Tesla’s Competitive Edge Over Waymo:

Tesla FSD also competes with Alphabet’s (GOOGL) Waymo. However, Tesla’s massive fleet (over 5 million FSD-capable vehicles, generating real-time data) gives it a huge advantage over Waymo (with only around 700 vehicles, operating in limited cities, and relying on costly “lidar” sensors). Estimates have FSD’s technology as potentially $10,000–$15,000 less expensive, per vehicle, versus Waymo’s $100,000+ retrofit efforts. Tesla CEO, Elon Musk, recently acknowledged this differentiator, saying:

"The car is very expensive, made in low volume. Teslas are probably cost 25% or 20% of what a Waymo costs and made in very high volume."

Tesla’s fleet size is a huge advantage, and its data and cost structure are hard to match. However Waymo’s has some advantages, such as regulatory approvals and operational robotaxis giving it a head start, but again Waymo is dramatically limited by scale as compared to FSD.

Valuing Tesla’s Potential:

Tesla appears extremely expensive on traditional valuation metrics. For example, with a current 123.9x forward price-to-earnings ratio, the stock appears most obviously overvalued to many investors.

However, at this point, Tesla’s valuation hinges on the success of FSD in disrupting the transportation market, which will potentially unlock massive growth, cash flows and profits.

And to put the importance of FSD in perspective, CEO Elon Musk recently said:

"The overwhelming focus is on solving full self-driving. That’s essential. It’s really the difference between Tesla being worth a lot of money or worth basically zero."

It seems clear that FSD is an existential opportunity for Tesla investors, but nonetheless it can also make sense to consider the company’s valuation using a sum-of-the parts approach, considering FSD, plus automotive and plus energy storage. For example, here are some very high-level “back-of-the-envelope” calculations:

Energy Storage Business: With $10.1B in 2024 revenue (10% of Tesla’s total revenue), up 67% from 2023, and with a gross margin of around 24.6%, we could assign a 10x price-to-sales multiple (this assumes many years of high growth) to value Tesla’s energy business at $101B. Obviously, this estimate includes a lot of big assumptions and “what-ifs,” as well as a high-degree of uncertainty.

Auto Business: Tesla’s auto segment (electric vehicles, leasing, and regulatory credits) generated $85.1B in 2024 revenue (85% of Tesla’s total revenue, up only 1% from 2023, and with a gross margin of around 17%), we could assign a 5x price-to-sales multiple (this assumes the company returns to growth as Musk refocuses on the business) to assign a value of $425.5B for Tesla’s auto business. Obviously, this includes a lot of assumptions (especially considering increasing competition from EV imitators and new competition).

FSD Business: Considering Tesla’s current market cap is around $1.2T, and we’ve estimated the other two main segments to be worth ~$526.5B ($425.5B + $101B), that means FSD must be worth at least $673.5B (1.2T - 526.5B) for the shares to be fairly valued. That is a huge (673.5 unicorns) valuation for a business segment that is not even a real business yet (it’s just an idea in “research & development”). However, considering our TAM estimates earlier, it’s not unreasonable to believe the FSD segment can be worth dramatically more than $673.5B if it is successful in the FSD robotaxi market.

For example, Tesla shares currently trade at ~$360, but Wall Street firms like Wedbush say it’s worth $390 (with a bull case of $650, citing optimism about FSD) and ARK Invest previously said it’s worth $2,600 per share by 2029 (estimating ~90% of Tesla’s enterprise value and earnings in 2029 will come from robotaxis). However, the average Wall Street price target is more bearish, recently at only $290 from the 36 firms covering it.

Clearly there is a lot of uncertainty and “what-ifs” involved in Tesla’s valuation, but if it is successful with FSD and the robotaxi industry, then the shares are worth dramatically more than the current price. Said differently, Tesla is a highly speculative investment that is absolutely not for everyone.

Risks

Obviously, Tesla shares face huge risks (most of them surrounding FSD execution risk) that investors need to consider. For example:

FSD Trust: Fully self-driving vehicles are pretty scary if you ask me. And it seems Tesla will need to achieve nearly perfect reliability to gain consumer trust. Any FSD accidents could quickly destroy investor confidence. However, this was the case when Tesla EV’s first entered the market, and several highly publicized car fires damaged the share price, but only temporarily. Tesla overcame those challenges.

FSD Adoption: Robotaxis require consumer buy-in. And cultural resistance still exists, despite the fact that FSD robotaxis will likely be significantly cheaper than Uber (potentially $0.50/mile vs. $2/mile), easier (and more cost effective) than car ownership, and “greener” (due to EVs being powered by increasingly cleaner energy grids).

Loss of Freedom: To some people, a car represents freedom. However, considering the extent to which FSD “stalks your every move” (it records everything) you are giving up some freedom. Not to mention, you’re also giving up control to a robot technology (i.e., “robo” taxi), and you’re still limited by EVs needing to be “plugged in” somewhere (instead of just fueling up and going). Nonetheless, compared to busses, trains, motor scooters and more, FSD robotaxis will be a big step up for many.

Regulatory Hurdles: FSD needs regulatory approval, akin to Tesla’s reliance on EV credits in its early days. Musk’s relationship with President Trump could expedite federal support, but local regulations vary, and FSD deployment could face significant delays.

The "Woke Mob": As mentioned, Musk is basically hated by a large percent of the U.S. population because of his political alignment (he has shown support for the current White House administration as per his previous leadership of so-called DOGE). Groups that once praised Musk for improving the environment by advancing the EV industry now reject anything he does. “DOGE is just becoming the whipping boy for everything” said Musk in a recent interview as he announced he would be scaling back his work leading DOGE (to focus more on his businesses) following big profit losses for Tesla.

Buffett’s Wisdom on Politics and Investing: Legendary investor, Warren Buffett, cautions against mixing politics with investments. For example, he once stated on CNBC:

“If you mix your politics with your investment decisions, you’re making a big mistake.”

Whether you agree or disagree with Buffett and/or Musk, Tesla’s stock has absolutely faced huge volatility and financial pressure due to politics. However, despite near-term pressures, if Tesla is successful with FSD, then its long-term fundamental attractiveness can drive the share price dramatically higher.

The Bottom Line

If you think Elon Musk is a horrible person, and you want him to fail into oblivion, I personally encourage you to proceed with that mission, legally and with civility. But regardless of your opinion, he is an exceptional example of following your dreams, to build something great, despite adversity and in the face of naysayers telling you it's not possible.

There is a good chance Musk will do it again with FSD, as he has presciently positioned himself for success in a large market opportunity that is about to materialize (and improve millions of lives) much quicker than many people envision. However, the risks are very real, and investors may want to consider owning shares as only one part of a prudently concentrated portfolio of high-growth disruptive opportunities.

At the end of the day, you need to do what is right for you (that’s how markets work). But if FSD succeeds, Musk may be about to “better the U.S. again.”

data.title: John Hancock U.S. Global Leaders Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly Commentary Highlights

  • U.S. stocks stumbled in the first quarter amid uncertainty about the effect of shifting U.S. trade policy on economic growth and inflation.
  • Growth stocks underperformed their value counterparts as large technology stocks that led the market's advance over the past two years were among the hardest hit in the sell-off.
  • The John Hancock U.S. Global Leaders Growth Fund (MUTF:USGLX) declined but held up better than its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Growth Index, due primarily to an underweight position in the information technology sector.

Market review and outlook

The U.S. stock market experienced meaningful volatility in the first quarter, driven largely by sweeping policy changes from the Trump administration. The most significant policy announcement was a series of tariffs on imports from various countries, including neighboring Canada and Mexico. The uncertainty created by these tariff announcements sent the broader equity market lower—after reaching an all-time high in mid-February, the S&P 500 Index (SP500), (SPX) declined by more than 8% through the end of the quarter. Value stocks outperformed growth shares by a wide margin amid a broad shift toward risk-off market sentiment.

The U.S. stock market entered the second quarter facing multiple crosscurrents: policy shifts reshaping trade relationships and threatening economic growth, technological innovations disrupting established business models, and valuations that suggest caution after years of exceptional returns. These market conditions typically favor the fund's investment approach, as markets tend to place higher premiums on sustainable, predictable growth during periods of uncertainty. We continue to find many attractively priced businesses with sustainable growth opportunities, pricing power, and operational resilience, and we expect these characteristics to become more valuable during periods when the market places higher premiums on the reliability and visibility of growth.

Contributors and detractors

The fund declined for the quarter but outperformed its benchmark. The change in market leadership, with information technology stocks lagging behind other market sectors, helped fuel the outperformance.

Leading individual performers in the portfolio included restaurant operator Yum! Brands, Inc. (YUM) and credit card processor Visa, Inc. (V) Yum! Brands reported earnings that exceeded market expectations as the company managed a challenging consumer environment in China and increased price competition in the United States. Meanwhile, Visa continued to deliver strong quarterly results, led by healthy revenue growth in value-added services.

On the downside, notable individual detractors from performance included online retailer Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and graphics processor manufacturer Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) Despite a robust quarterly earnings report by Amazon.com, investors reacted negatively to an increase in capital expenditures driven by Al initiatives. Nvidia was adversely affected by Al advancements introduced by Chinese company DEEPSEEK that challenged core assumptions about the power consumption and infrastructure investments needed.

Portfolio changes

We added no new companies to the fund in the first quarter, but we eliminated one portfolio holding—market data and analytics provider MSCI, Inc. The company faced client headwinds and an uncertain market environment, so we chose to sell the position and reallocate the proceeds to more attractively valued holdings in the portfolio.

data.title: LVMH: Decline Reflects Two Concerns
data.description:
LVMH Moët Hennessy - Louis Vuitton, Société Européenne (OTCPK:LVMHF) has reported weak sales. The luxury powerhouse’s Q1 and outlook reflect persisting luxury market weakness. More concerningly, LVMH’s historically strong performance in luxury fashion is under question, as some of LVMH’s critical fashion brands are losing share to competitors.

I initiated LVMH at a Hold rating in my initial February article on the stock, titled “LVMH: Only Hermès Can Rival In Luxury Fashion Prestige”. Due to the aforementioned issues, the stock has since lost -23% of its value, underperforming the S&P 500’s decline of -2%. In euros, LVMH’s primary currency, the decline has been even more pronounced.

Q1 Sales: Luxury Slowdown Continues, LVMH’s Market Share Declines

LVMH’s Q1 sales were weak. Total revenues declined by -3% organically to €20.3 billion, reflecting a weak -5% revenue growth performance in the core Fashion & Leather Goods segment and an even sharper -9% organic decline in Wines & Spirits revenues. The results missed the market’s expectations notably, causing LVMH’s stock to decline and the market to revisit expectations for the year. The underlying reason for LVMH’s weak performance is twofold.

The most common narrative is the luxury market’s continued weakness - after the luxury market was already weak in 2024, contributing to LVMH’s slow performance in the last year, the trend has continued into 2025 as well. A weak economy in, especially China, continues to weigh on the luxury industry, and LVMH’s -11% sales decline in Asia (excl. Japan) clearly reflects the fact. The Japanese market’s -1% sales decline also echoes the Chinese consumer’s weakness from weaker tourism-related revenues, as LVMH seemed to note in the Q1 earnings call. LVMH’s revenues also declined by -3% in the US, albeit the slowdown was related by LVMH to distribution normalization. As tariffs go into effect and consumers’ discretionary spending has shifted, the luxury downturn is expected to continue past Q1 as well. LVMH’s earnings call echoed continued macroeconomic uncertainty in the outlook, setting up weak 2025 growth.

The spirits industry has also remained very weak, albeit the decline seems to be more secular as younger people drink less. Worldwide spirits sales volumes are projected to stagnate and even slowly decline.

The other, more concerning reason for LVMH’s decline is the company’s market share performance in luxury fashion. LVMH has historically been a very strong fashion powerhouse, reporting significant market share gains. According to luxury fashion industry data that I gathered, LVMH’s LTM market share grew from approximately 28.80% in Q1’19 to a peak of 39.30% in Q4’2023 in a consistent manner. Since, the market share has now started to cool down in a concerning manner – while initially still near-stable, LVMH has now lost 60 basis points of market share within a year. The market share stood at 38.60% on an LTM basis after Q1. A shift from market share gains into declines is detrimental to long-term growth expectations.

Notably, Hermès (OTCPK:HESAY), Ralph Lauren (RL), Prada (OTCPK:PRDSY), Tapestry (TPR), and some other smaller fashion houses have taken market share from LVMH; against LVMH’s Q1 revenue decline, Hermès reported a strong 7% organic revenue growth despite a slow sales environment. Prada’s revenues grew by an even faster 13%.

The company’s fashion brand performance seems to be a cause for some concern. Although the most important Louis Vuitton brand has continued to generate healthy interest according to Google search volumes, Dior has shown a notably weaker performance in early 2025 – after gaining relevance in 2024, Dior has lost some back in 2025. Fendi has continued to lose relevance at quite a fast pace. The lost relevance hasn’t gone to LVMH’s other brands, but rather Hermès’, Ralph Lauren’s, and others’ topline performances.

Although concerning, I don’t believe the market share decline to be too alarming yet. LVMH faced a slowdown in market share growth during 2021, but ultimately returned back to stronger market share gains afterwards; fashion trends can come in cycles, and the recent decline shouldn’t be extrapolated too far for now. LVMH’s brand portfolio remains very healthy compared to for example, Kering’s (OTCPK:PPRUF) Gucci downfall. The luxury fashion industry’s great margins make a moderate revenue decline relatively inconsequential on total earnings compared to typical apparel companies.

Valuation: LVMH's Decline Has Been Overpronounced

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for LVMH to reflect weaker growth. I now estimate much weaker 2025 growth at -1.5%. Afterwards, while I still anticipate a luxury market recovery, I have slightly lowered my growth expectations due to LVMH’s market share pressure. I now assume a total 2024-2034 revenue CAGR of 2.7%. I previously expected a 4.1% CAGR.

Due to weaker sales, I also slightly lowered my long-term EBIT margin expectation to 23% from 24% previously. LVMH’s cash flow should remain very healthy.

The estimates put LVMH’s fair value estimate at $676.4, 22% above the stock price at the time of writing; I believe that LVMH’s stock decline has been over-pronounced. Although I now expect lower earnings, the total impact of temporary luxury market weakness and moderate market share pressure doesn’t completely deteriorate the investment’s prospects.

The fair value estimate is down from $737.7 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.43%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I again estimate a 5.45% interest rate and 8% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year EUR swap yield of 2.53% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 5.13% is Professor Aswath Damodaran's most recent January estimates for France. I have kept LVMH’s beta estimate at 0.99. With a liquidity premium of 0.1%, the cost of equity stands at 7.71% and the WACC at 7.43%.

Takeaway

LVMH’s sales performance has trailed for two main reasons – a continued downturn in the luxury market and increased market share pressure in LVMH’s luxury fashion segment. The second reason weighs more, pressuring long-term growth expectations. For now, I don’t believe that the short-term hiccup in market share gains is cause for too much concern, though – the market’s strong reaction has likely been over-pronounced. I’m still cautious and reiterate LVMH at a Hold rating.

data.title: John Hancock Preferred Income ETF Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary Highlights

  • The U.S. bond market rose in the first quarter as bond yields declined sharply.
  • The John Hancock Preferred Income ETF fund (NYSEARCA:JHPI) underperformed its benchmark, the ICE BofA U.S. All Capital Securities Index.
  • Security selection within the communications sector was a positive contributor to performance, while security selection within the electric utilities sector was a detractor.

Market review and outlook

U.S. bonds recorded positive returns in the first quarter. The bond market experienced a sell-off early on driven by strong economic data, including a better-than-expected jobs report in January. However, bond yields subsequently declined sharply as signs of slowing economic growth emerged, highlighted by a significant drop in retail sales and the lowest consumer sentiment reading since November 2022. Furthermore, changing U.S. trade and tariff policies created considerable uncertainty, resulting in risk-off market sentiment during the latter half of the quarter. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained short-term interest rates throughout the quarter while revising its economic projections downward and raising its inflation forecasts.

In this context, bond yields fell across the board, with intermediate-term bond yields decreasing the most. Sector performance mirrored the risk-off sentiment, with U.S. Treasury securities and government agency residential mortgage-backed securities delivering the best returns. In contrast, high-yield corporate bonds and asset-backed securities lagged for the quarter.

With the shift in market focus from the Fed and inflation to tariffs and implications for growth, interest rates rallied sharply and credit spreads weakened. We anticipate that the Fed will continue to depend on data and not react impulsively to the Trump administration's policy announcements. While broader economic data has softened somewhat, labor markets remain stable and inflation appears to be broadly contained, although still above the Fed's target.

Contributors and detractors

The fund underperformed the overall preferred securities market in the first quarter. Positive contributors to performance included security selection in the communications sector as well as the banking and insurance sectors.

The portfolio's detractors included security selection in the electric utilities and brokerage asset manager sectors. Furthermore, the portfolio's security selection in preferred stock was the top asset class detractor.

Portfolio positioning

We favor defensive sectors, such as electric utilities, which have demonstrated resilience during stressful periods and provided solid downside protection. This sector is primarily insulated from the current tariff war. Most of the preferred universe consists of financials, which are expected to experience little to no direct effect from tariffs, particularly in the insurance sector. There are still attractive opportunities to generate income within credit and spread sectors, with the potential for spread compression and limited risk of permanent capital impairment.

data.title: Hormel Foods: No Rush To Buy, Given Ambitious Guidance
data.description:
Shares of Hormel Foods (NYSE:HRL) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing 10%. The company has struggled with rising input costs that have been difficult to pass on to customers without weakening volumes. Additionally, its marquee Planters brand has struggled to maintain relevance with younger consumers. While it has been a disappointing year for its investors, shares did rise 1% on Thursday despite reporting mixed Q2 results, suggesting the bad news may be factored into valuation. With updated financials, now is a good time to see if HRL is worth buying. There are tentative signs that fundamentals are turning, but not enough to add shares.

In the company’s fiscal second quarter, Hormel earned $0.35, which beat estimates by a penny, as revenue was flat year over year at $2.9 billion. EPS was down about 8% from last year. Organic sales growth was 1%, though volumes were down 7%. Hormel has been able to raise prices to increase revenue and recapture some cost, but clearly, consumers remain very price sensitive, leading to a nearly equally sized drop-off in volumes. This reflects a difficult operating environment with limited discretionary income. On the earnings call, management said the retail environment is “choppy” with a strained consumer, which, I believe, is a fair description of the environment.

Lower volumes reduced operating efficiencies, and as such, Hormel reported just a 16.7% gross margin. Even though sales rose by $11 million, gross profit fell by $19 million, reflecting some of these pressures. The company is closing a plant in California, moving that production to its two remaining plants, which should help improve unit costs. Given lower gross profit, operating income fell by $17 million to $248 million as margins compressed 50bps to 8.6%.

These pressures came even as there are some tangible benefits to management’s cost-cutting effort, the transformation and modernize (T&M) initiative. Management reiterated $100-$150 million of benefits from this program this year. Validating this claim, SG&A was $15 million lower at $251 million. One headwind for activity in the quarter was that HRL pulled back on advertising ahead of a large push in H2. Advertising spending was $36 million, down $8 million from last year. It expects advertising costs to rise double-digits in H2, largely to support new products from Planters, SPAM, and other key brands.

Looking at segment results, retail accounts for 60% of the business. Here, sales were flat despite a 7% drop in volume, thanks to price increases. Thanks to cost cuts from its T&M program, profits rose by 4%. Lower commodity shipments drove the sales decline. Importantly, Planters is showing sequential improvement (though still declining), and its Applegate brand reaccelerated to 6% sales growth. Its Mexican offerings are also growing, thanks to strength from Herdez.

Planters has been posting declines, but management expects sales growth to turn positive later this year, given its new marketing push. New product launches, including its nut “duo” packs, have helped to liven the brand, and an ongoing focus on protein-oriented and healthy snacking should be a favorable trend for the brand. Applegate has seen strong take-up of its breakfast offerings.

Foodservice volume was down 1%, excluding the impact of divestitures. Due to price increases, organic net sales rose by 4% to $936 million. Profits fell by 4%, given margin pressure, and it is seeing end-market softness. This unit is particularly exposed to traffic at convenience stores, and consumer spending on snacks here can be quite discretionary. Given the tepid consumer environment, I struggle to see a case for much acceleration here.

Finally, international volume rose 9%, but profits fell by 21%. It has seen solid exports and strong growth in China, in particular, where it operates locally. Brazil was a source of weakness. This unit is just about 7% of the business, and so the ultimate impact is negligible. The US is a net exporter of agriculture products, so insofar as trade tensions lead to countervailing duties, that could pose a risk. Importantly, it has a local presence within China, which insulates it from some of these trade pressures.

One key segment for the business is Turkey, where supply has been a source of pressure, which has weakened margins. It has taken time to pass increases on to consumers, though its shift towards value-add products has helped to insulate it somewhat. Management is seeing some sequential improvement in pricing and believes most costs will be recaptured during the key “whole bird” Thanksgiving selling season.

Alongside results, management tightened its guidance range. It now expects full year sales growth to be 2-3% and anticipates that tariffs will be just a 1 to 2 cent headwind. Interestingly, while it raised the bottom of its sales estimates, it lowered the top of earnings estimates, suggesting greater margin pressures than previously expected, partly due to tariffs.

Frankly, even with the top end lowered a bit, this is a fairly ambitious guide, with management clearly expecting growth to accelerate in H2. Its implied H2 guide assumes over 22% operating income growth from last year, or over $700 million. It is forecasting at least $0.88 in H2 earnings from $0.70 in H1. Management is quite clearly betting on the recent momentum in Planters and turkey pricing to accelerate, aided by a more aggressive marketing stance. Frankly, I think there is still some skepticism on the ability to deliver this level of acceleration, and otherwise, I expect shares would have reacted more positively. This skepticism is merited

It remains an open question whether HRL can deliver enough margin expansion to flex up operating income that much, especially with increased promotional activity. Indeed, you can see that producer prices (its costs) are now rising more quickly than consumer prices. You can see from the below how input prices began pressuring margins in 2022, then producers recouped this. But now, we are entering a more difficult margin environment.

Given the difficulty Hormel has had pushing higher input costs on to consumers without sacrificing volumes, I am concerned about the margin backdrop, especially given the need to see accelerating sales growth to meet its guidance targets. Given the summer driving season boosting c-store activity and seasonality around turkey, there should be H2 improvement relative to H1, but I see full year EPS as likely to be in the $1.53-$1.63 range, bracketing the low end of guidance.

Now, I would note that Hormel has a strong balance sheet, with $670 million of cash and $2.9 billion of debt. Especially as pre-summer inventory builds reverse, we should see strong free cash flow generation over the balance of the year, and with debt already at the low end of target, Hormel has capacity for bolt-on M&A or further dividend growth. Shares have a 3.9% dividend yield. Management is committed to dividend growth, with 59 years of increase. I expect that streak to continue

With $1.62 of forward earnings power, I see Hormel trading with an ~18.7x multiple. This is not a compelling entry point, especially for a stock that is flat over the past decade and down over a third in the past five years. Hormel’s guidance is aggressive, and I see downside risk, given rising input costs and questions about how quickly it can reaccelerate Planters' sales. Given its balance sheet and the likelihood of some improvement, I do not see a rush to sell shares, and ~$30 strikes me as fair value for a low-growth company with a solid balance sheet and secure dividend. Still, I would look elsewhere for upside and view shares as a “hold.”
